content:
3 min read
Just now

--

There’s a piece of misinformation traveling through boardrooms right now, and it sounds like relief: “The EU AI Act deadline got pushed back.”
It’s half true. And the half that’s false could cost a company up to €35 million.
Here’s what actually happened. In May 2026, the EU reached provisional agreement on a package called the Digital Omnibus — a simplification effort that touched the AI Act’s timeline. For most of the past two years, August 2, 2026 was treated as one monolithic deadline. The Omnibus cleaved it into two very different categories: obligations that were deferred, and obligations that were not.
What moved: The high-risk AI system rules — the heavy compliance regime tied to Annex III — were pushed to December 2027. That’s genuine breathing room for companies building high-risk systems, and it was a sensible move given the supporting standards weren’t ready.
What did not move: Article 50 — the transparency obligations — stayed exactly where it was. August 2, 2026. And this is the part that matters, because Article 50 is the single most broadly applicable obligation in the entire Act. It keys off what an AI system does, not how risky it’s classified. If a system generates content, interacts with people as a chatbot, or produces synthetic media, Article 50 applies.
Even more important: August 2 isn’t just when the rules apply. It’s when regulators gain the power to fine for violating them. The obligation and the enforcement machinery arrive together. There is no grace period where the rule exists but the penalty doesn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Who this hits — and it’s a much wider net than “high-risk”:
Any company using generative AI to produce text, images, audio, or video carries a marking obligation. Any company running a customer-facing chatbot must disclose it. Any organization publishing AI-generated content on matters of public interest, or deploying deepfake-capable tools, falls under the disclosure rules. That’s not a narrow set of AI labs — that’s marketing teams, media companies, customer service operations, and the long tail of businesses that quietly turned on an “AI feature” somewhere.
The penalty for getting it wrong reaches €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher. And it applies regardless of where the company is headquartered, as long as its AI output reaches EU users.
The trap that’s forming right now:
Organizations that read the headlines about “deadlines being delayed” and quietly stood down their AI Act preparation have made a serious mistake. They deferred the wrong thing in their heads. The high-risk regime moving does not translate into breathing room for a company publishing AI-generated marketing content — for them, the obligation that’s most likely to apply, and the fine that backs it, are both on the unmoved part of the calendar.
We’re roughly a month out. August 2, 2026.
This is the same lesson underneath every governance failure I write about: the danger is rarely the technology itself. It’s misreading where the line falls — assuming you’re outside the scope when you’re squarely inside it. A company that governs AI well isn’t one that reacted to a headline. It’s one that read the actual obligation, mapped it to what it actually does, and prepared for the deadline that was always going to stand.
Questions worth asking inside your organization this month:

• Do we use AI to generate any content, or run any chatbot that interacts with customers?

• If so, do we have Article 50 disclosure and marking handled before August 2?

• Did we mistakenly stand down our AI Act work because we heard “the deadline moved”?

• Does any of our AI output reach EU users — making this our obligation regardless of where we’re based?

• Who owns this deadline internally? If the answer is no one, that’s the finding.
Solutions by Jewel is an AI governance advisory firm. We help organizations read the regulatory line correctly — and build the frameworks and oversight to stay on the right side of it
